heroOverview: Obesity is a chronic disease of excess body fat accumulation sufficient to impair health, defined by a body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or above in adults. It affects roughly 650 million adults worldwide and 13% of the global adult population as of the most recent WHO estimates — a prevalence that has tripled since 1975. Excess adiposity drives a cascade of metabolic, cardiovascular, musculoskeletal, and psychological consequences, with type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease among the most clinically significant. Obesity is not simply a behavioural failing; it results from complex interactions among genetics, environment, gut microbiome, sleep, endocrine function, and psychological factors. Effective management combines dietary change, increased physical activity, behavioural therapy, and in appropriate patients, pharmacotherapy or bariatric surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 650 million adults (13% of global adult population) have obesity; over 1 billion adults are overweight or obese (WHO, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Prevalence is highest in adults aged 40-59; rates are rising fastest in low- and middle-income countries
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of onset of obesity-related comorbidities typically in the fourth or fifth decade, though childhood obesity is increasing
keyStats.globalCases: 4 million deaths per year attributable to overweight and obesity globally (GBD, 2023)

definition: Obesity (ICD-10: E66.9) is defined as abnormal or excessive fat accumulation that presents a risk to health. The World Health Organization defines it as a BMI of 30 kg/m² or above in adults; BMI 25-29.9 defines overweight. Class I obesity spans BMI 30-34.9, class II 35-39.9, and class III (severe obesity) 40 or above. BMI is a convenient population screening tool but does not directly measure body fat; waist circumference above 94 cm in men and 80 cm in women (WHO thresholds for European populations) better reflects central adiposity, which correlates more strongly with cardiometabolic risk than total BMI alone. Asian populations have higher cardiometabolic risk at lower BMI thresholds, with the WHO suggesting cut-offs of 23 for overweight and 27.5 for obesity. Obesity results from sustained positive energy balance — energy intake chronically exceeding expenditure — driven by biological, behavioural, and environmental factors. It is recognised by major medical organisations including the American Medical Association, WHO, and the UK NICE as a disease rather than a lifestyle choice. It falls under endocrinology for metabolic work-up, but management is typically multidisciplinary.

typesClassification.description: BMI 30-34.9 kg/m²; associated with moderate increases in cardiometabolic and musculoskeletal risk relative to healthy weight.
BMI 35-39.9 kg/m²; substantially elevated risk of type 2 diabetes, sleep apnoea, and joint disease; pharmacotherapy is typically considered at this stage.
BMI 40 kg/m² or above; highest risk category, associated with dramatically increased all-cause mortality; bariatric surgery is the most effective treatment at this severity.
Excess fat concentrated in the abdomen, independently predictive of cardiometabolic disease even at lower total BMI; assessed by waist circumference or waist-to-hip ratio.
A minority of obese individuals without the typical metabolic abnormalities; long-term follow-up studies suggest this is often a transitional state rather than a stable benign phenotype.

primarySymptoms: Excess body weight or visible fat accumulation, often concentrated in the abdomen, hips, and thighs depending on sex and fat distribution pattern.
Shortness of breath with mild to moderate exertion, resulting from the mechanical load of excess weight on the respiratory system.
Fatigue and low energy that persists with adequate sleep, partly from the increased metabolic and mechanical cost of carrying excess mass.
Joint pain, particularly in the knees, hips, and lower back, from abnormal mechanical loading of weight-bearing joints.
Sleep disruption or loud snoring, often related to obstructive sleep apnoea from excess upper airway soft tissue.
Excessive sweating and heat intolerance, as greater body mass increases baseline metabolic heat production.
Skin changes including acanthosis nigricans — velvety, dark patches in skin folds — which signal insulin resistance.
Intertrigo — skin inflammation, redness, and sometimes infection in skin folds where surfaces rub together.
Gastro-oesophageal reflux and heartburn, which are significantly more prevalent with obesity due to increased intra-abdominal pressure.
Reduced mobility and difficulty with activities of daily living as BMI increases above 35 kg/m².
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive weight gain of more than 5-10% of body weight over a year without an intentional cause.
Increasing waist circumference beyond threshold values, even before BMI enters the obese range.
New snoring or daytime sleepiness, suggesting early obstructive sleep apnoea.
Fasting glucose trending toward the pre-diabetes range on routine blood tests.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe chest pain or difficulty breathing — obesity is a major risk factor for myocardial infarction and pulmonary embolism.
Excessive daytime sleepiness with episodes of stopping breathing during sleep — severe obstructive sleep apnoea requires urgent assessment.
Obesity hypoventilation syndrome presenting as drowsiness, confusion, and low oxygen saturation at rest.
Diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state in a patient with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes and obesity.

causes.description: Sustained intake of energy-dense, ultra-processed foods — high in refined carbohydrates, saturated fat, and free sugars — exceeds metabolic needs; ultra-processed food consumption is strongly correlated with obesity prevalence across populations and promotes overconsumption partly through disrupting satiety signalling.

Reduced non-exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT) and deliberate exercise lower total energy expenditure; modern occupational and leisure patterns involve prolonged sitting, reducing daily energy expenditure by hundreds of kilocalories compared with more active lifestyles.

Twin studies estimate heritability of BMI at 40-70%; common genetic variants identified through genome-wide association studies largely affect appetite regulation and the melanocortin pathway; rare monogenic forms (e.g. MC4R mutation, leptin deficiency) cause severe early-onset obesity but account for fewer than 5% of cases.

Conditions including hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, and hypothalamic damage from tumours or injury impair energy balance regulation and can drive weight gain independent of diet; these are identified on metabolic screening.

causes.cause: Sleep insufficiency and poor sleep quality
causes.description: Short sleep duration consistently predicts weight gain in longitudinal studies; sleep deprivation elevates ghrelin (hunger hormone), suppresses leptin (satiety hormone), increases preference for calorie-dense foods, and reduces activity levels the following day.

Many widely prescribed drugs cause clinically significant weight gain, including antipsychotics (olanzapine, clozapine), antidepressants (mirtazapine, tricyclics), mood stabilisers (valproate, lithium), insulin and sulfonylureas, corticosteroids, and some antihypertensives; medication-related obesity should be identified and alternatives sought where possible.

Emerging evidence shows that altered gut bacterial composition affects energy harvest from food, bile acid metabolism, and inflammatory signalling; while causality is not firmly established in humans, microbiome differences between obese and lean individuals are reproducible and are a focus of active research.

riskFactors.factor: High intake of ultra-processed foods and sugar-sweetened beverages
riskFactors.description: Ultra-processed foods are engineered to be hyper-palatable and promote overconsumption; sugar-sweetened beverages add liquid calories without triggering normal satiety, and high intake increases obesity risk by 26-31% in meta-analyses.

Adults who meet WHO guidelines of 150-300 minutes of moderate activity per week have substantially lower obesity rates; for every hour of daily TV viewing, mean BMI increases by about 0.25 kg/m² in population studies.

Sleeping fewer than seven hours per night is independently associated with a roughly 30% higher risk of obesity in meta-analyses; restoring adequate sleep reduces appetite and improves metabolic function.
Having two obese parents raises a child's lifetime obesity risk roughly tenfold; the effect reflects both shared genetics and shared environment.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and food environment
riskFactors.description: Low income, limited access to fresh food, and food-insecure environments strongly predict obesity; communities with high density of fast-food outlets and low density of green space have measurably higher obesity rates.

Depression and anxiety disorder are associated with a 20-40% higher obesity risk, driven by changes in eating behaviour, reduced activity, and weight-promoting medications; the relationship is bidirectional.
Hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, PCOS, and hypothalamic dysfunction directly promote fat accumulation; these are present in a minority of obese patients but must be excluded during evaluation.
Drug-induced weight gain is common and often overlooked; reviewing the medication list for alternatives to weight-promoting agents is an important early intervention.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-59, among whom prevalence peaks in most high-income countries
Women during and after menopause, with hormonal shifts favouring central fat redistribution
People of low socioeconomic status and those living in food-insecure environments
People with mental health conditions, particularly depression and schizophrenia
Indigenous populations and certain ethnic groups with higher genetic susceptibility
Children and adolescents, among whom global prevalence has risen sharply since the 1990s
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of obesity in adults is based on BMI calculation (weight in kg divided by height in metres squared) with a threshold of 30 kg/m². BMI should be interpreted alongside waist circumference, which provides independent cardiovascular risk information — high risk begins at waist circumference above 94 cm (men) or 80 cm (women) by WHO standards for European populations. A full assessment evaluates adiposity-related complications: fasting glucose and HbA1c for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes, fasting lipid profile for dyslipidaemia, blood pressure for hypertension, liver enzymes for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, thyroid-stimulating hormone to exclude hypothyroidism, and a sleep history with polysomnography if sleep apnoea is suspected. Causes of secondary obesity — Cushing's syndrome, hypothyroidism, hypothalamic disease, and medication side effects — should be considered, particularly in patients with rapid or atypical weight gain. NICE recommends a structured assessment of dietary habits, physical activity levels, and psychological factors as part of the initial consultation. In clinical practice, the Edmonton Obesity Staging System, which classifies patients by presence and severity of obesity-related disease rather than BMI alone, is increasingly used to prioritise treatment intensity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies obesity severity and estimates cardiometabolic risk; waist circumference adds independent risk information beyond BMI.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Height and weight are measured in the clinic; waist circumference is measured with a tape at the level of the umbilicus after normal exhalation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes, which affect roughly 30-40% of people with class II-III obesity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw after an eight-hour fast; HbA1c does not require fasting. Results are typically available within one to two days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies dyslipidaemia — elevated LDL, triglycerides, or low HDL — that amplifies cardiovascular risk in obesity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw after an eight-hour fast; results available the same or next day.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and liver ultrasound
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, present in approximately 30-40% of patients with obesity, which can progress to cirrhosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn for enzymes; liver ultrasound is painless and takes 15-20 minutes with a gel-covered probe on the abdomen.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypothyroidism as a secondary cause of weight gain; a reasonable baseline test in new presentations of obesity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw; results available within one day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses obstructive sleep apnoea, present in up to 40% of adults with obesity, which worsens cardiometabolic risk and disrupts weight management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An overnight study either at a sleep centre or at home with a portable device, measuring oxygen levels, breathing effort, and body position during sleep.

treatmentOverview: Obesity management requires a sustained, multidisciplinary approach tailored to each patient's severity, comorbidities, and readiness to change. The NICE and AHA/ACC/AOS guidelines consistently recommend behavioural and lifestyle intervention as the foundation: dietary modification targeting a 500-600 kcal daily deficit, combined with physical activity (at least 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week) and structured behavioural therapy, achieves around 5-10% weight loss over 6-12 months. A 5-10% reduction is clinically meaningful — it reduces type 2 diabetes risk by roughly 50%, lowers blood pressure by 3-5 mmHg, and improves sleep apnoea severity. For patients with a BMI of 30 or above who have not achieved adequate weight loss with lifestyle changes, pharmacotherapy is an option. GLP-1 receptor agonists, particularly semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) at high dose and tirzepatide, have transformed pharmacotherapy, achieving 15-20% weight loss in clinical trials — far more than older agents. Older approved drugs include orlistat, phentermine-topiramate, and naltrexone-bupropion. For patients with BMI above 40, or above 35 with significant comorbidities, bariatric surgery is the most effective long-term treatment, with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy each achieving 25-35% total body weight loss and high rates of type 2 diabetes remission. Obesity is a chronic relapsing disease; treatment must be long-term and relapse is expected if interventions are withdrawn.

medicalTreatments.name: Semaglutide (GLP-1 receptor agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: A once-weekly subcutaneous injection that acts on hypothalamic GLP-1 receptors to suppress appetite and delay gastric emptying; approved at 2.4 mg weekly (Wegovy) for obesity management in adults with BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with a weight-related comorbidity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the STEP trials, 68-week treatment achieved mean weight loss of approximately 15% of body weight, with 32% of patients losing 20% or more.

medicalTreatments.name: Tirzepatide (GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: A once-weekly injection targeting both GIP and GLP-1 receptors, approved for obesity in several countries; produces greater average weight loss than semaglutide in head-to-head data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the SURMOUNT-1 trial, 72-week treatment produced mean weight loss of approximately 20-22% at the highest dose, with over 50% of patients losing 20% or more.

medicalTreatments.name: Orlistat
medicalTreatments.description: An oral lipase inhibitor that blocks approximately 30% of dietary fat absorption; available over the counter at 60 mg and by prescription at 120 mg three times daily with meals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces mean weight loss of approximately 3-5 kg more than placebo over one year; reduces incidence of type 2 diabetes by 37% in the XENDOS trial over four years.

medicalTreatments.name: Naltrexone-bupropion (Contrave/Mysimba)
medicalTreatments.description: A combination oral tablet affecting hypothalamic and mesolimbic reward circuits to reduce appetite and food cravings; taken twice daily with titration over four weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves approximately 5-6% weight loss beyond placebo over one year in clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured behavioural weight management programme
medicalTreatments.description: A multicomponent programme combining dietary education, physical activity coaching, and cognitive-behavioural therapy delivered over at least 12 weeks; the basis of all obesity management recommendations and often a prerequisite for pharmacotherapy or surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves 5-10% weight loss in 6-12 months in approximately 50-60% of participants; sustained only with ongoing support.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Approximately 80% of the stomach is surgically removed, creating a sleeve-shaped stomach with roughly 15% of original capacity; this restricts food intake and substantially lowers ghrelin production, reducing appetite.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mean total body weight loss of approximately 25-30% at five years; type 2 diabetes remission in 50-60% of patients at one year.

surgicalOptions.name: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass
surgicalOptions.description: The stomach is divided to create a small pouch connected directly to the mid-small intestine, bypassing the lower stomach and upper small intestine; combines restriction with hormonal changes favouring satiety and glucose control.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mean total body weight loss of approximately 30-35% at five years; type 2 diabetes remission in approximately 70-80% of patients at one year.

surgicalOptions.name: Adjustable gastric band
surgicalOptions.description: A silicone band placed laparoscopically around the upper stomach to create a small pouch; adjustable via a subcutaneous port but increasingly replaced by sleeve gastrectomy due to higher reoperation rates.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mean total body weight loss of approximately 15-20% at five years; lower complication rates but lower efficacy and higher long-term revision rates than sleeve or bypass.

alternativeTreatments.name: Very low calorie diet (VLCD)
alternativeTreatments.description: Formula diets providing 800 kcal per day or less, used under medical supervision for rapid weight loss as a bridge to longer-term management; the DiRECT trial showed nearly half of participants achieved remission of type 2 diabetes with a total diet replacement approach.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based interventions
alternativeTreatments.description: Mindful eating and stress-reduction programmes can reduce emotional and binge eating, contributing modestly to weight management as an adjunct to standard treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be involved when secondary causes of obesity are suspected (Cushing's syndrome, hypothyroidism, hypothalamic disease), when standard management has failed, or when evaluating suitability for pharmacotherapy or surgical referral. Bariatric surgery assessment requires a multidisciplinary team including a surgeon, dietitian, psychologist, and physician. Patients with multiple obesity-related comorbidities benefit from coordinated specialist input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist or obesity medicine specialist experienced in comprehensive metabolic assessment and familiar with current pharmacotherapy including GLP-1 agonists. For surgical options, seek a bariatric unit with high volume (at least 50 procedures per surgeon per year), a multidisciplinary team, and long-term follow-up programmes. Psychological support from a clinician experienced in health behaviour change is a critical adjunct at every stage.
preventionStrategies: Prioritise whole foods over ultra-processed products; diets high in vegetables, legumes, whole grains, and lean protein consistently show lower obesity rates across populations.
Aim for at least 150-300 minutes of moderate physical activity per week and reduce daily sitting time with movement breaks.
Protect sleep — adults should target seven to nine hours; addressing sleep disorders such as obstructive sleep apnoea aids weight management.
Manage stress through exercise, social support, and formal psychological techniques, as chronic stress raises cortisol and drives caloric overconsumption.
Review all medications with a prescriber to identify and replace weight-promoting drugs where clinically possible.
Implement weight management at the earliest sign of gain — intervening at BMI 27-29 is more effective than waiting until class II or III obesity develops.
lifestyleModifications: Prioritise dietary pattern over single nutrient restriction — Mediterranean, DASH, and whole-food plant-based approaches all produce sustained weight loss and metabolic benefit.
Eat slowly and without distraction; it takes roughly 20 minutes for satiety hormones to signal fullness, so slowing the pace of eating reduces caloric intake.
Keep a food and activity diary — self-monitoring is one of the strongest behavioural predictors of long-term weight loss maintenance.
Build incidental activity into daily routine — walking meetings, parking farther away, using stairs — as non-exercise activity thermogenesis is a major component of total expenditure.
Set realistic, specific behavioural goals rather than weight-focused goals alone; process goals (cooking at home five times a week) are more actionable than outcome goals.
Enlist social support — involving a partner or joining a structured group programme improves long-term outcomes.

recommended: Non-starchy vegetables and fruits as the majority of dietary volume
Legumes, beans, and lentils as high-satiety, high-fibre protein sources
Whole grains such as oats, barley, and quinoa over refined carbohydrates
Lean proteins including poultry, fish, eggs, and low-fat dairy
Unsaturated fats from olive oil, avocado, and nuts in moderate quantities
avoid: Sugar-sweetened beverages, including juices, sodas, and energy drinks
Ultra-processed snack foods engineered for overconsumption
Large portion sizes, particularly of energy-dense foods
Frequent takeaway and fast-food meals, which consistently provide more calories and less fibre than home-cooked food

exerciseGuidelines: WHO guidelines recommend 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) or 75-150 minutes of vigorous activity per week, plus muscle-strengthening exercises twice weekly. For weight loss, higher volumes — 250-300 minutes per week — produce greater benefit. Both aerobic and resistance training are valuable; resistance training preserves lean muscle mass during weight loss, which helps maintain resting metabolic rate. People with joint pain or limited mobility should start with low-impact activities and progress gradually; hydrotherapy is particularly well tolerated at higher BMI.
dailyManagement: Weigh yourself weekly rather than daily to track meaningful trends without unhelpful noise from fluid fluctuations.
Take any prescribed medication consistently; do not stop GLP-1 agonists or orlistat without discussing with a prescriber, as weight typically returns after cessation.
Attend scheduled follow-up appointments for monitoring of blood pressure, glucose, lipids, and liver function.
Address mental health — if mood or anxiety is deteriorating, contact a prescriber promptly, as undertreated mental health impedes weight management.
Plan meals in advance to reduce impulsive food choices driven by hunger and environmental cues.
prognosis: Obesity is a chronic, relapsing condition. With lifestyle intervention alone, most adults can achieve 5-10% weight loss, which produces meaningful reductions in diabetes risk (approximately 50%), blood pressure, and sleep apnoea severity. However, more than 80% of people who lose weight through lifestyle change alone regain it within five years, reflecting the powerful biological drives that defend prior body weight. Pharmacotherapy with GLP-1 agonists can now achieve and maintain 15-22% weight loss while treatment continues, substantially altering the natural history. Bariatric surgery produces the most durable weight loss (25-35% at five years) and is the only intervention with robust evidence for long-term type 2 diabetes remission. Mortality from obesity-related conditions including cardiovascular disease and certain cancers is elevated at BMI above 30 and rises steeply above 40; a 5-10% weight loss reduces all-cause mortality risk measurably even when BMI remains above 30.
recoveryTimeline: Weight loss with lifestyle intervention typically proceeds at 0.5-1 kg per week during the active phase. Meaningful clinical benefit (5% body weight loss) is often achieved within eight to twelve weeks of a structured programme. Pharmacotherapy with GLP-1 agonists shows rapid early weight loss in the first 12-20 weeks, with continued but slower loss to a plateau at around 60-72 weeks. Bariatric surgery patients lose weight most rapidly in the first three to six months, with most weight loss completed by 12-18 months; metabolic improvements in blood glucose and blood pressure often occur within days to weeks of surgery, before substantial weight loss is achieved.
complications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — risk is approximately three times higher in people with obesity compared to those of healthy weight; even modest weight loss reduces progression from prediabetes by 50-58%.
Coronary artery disease and heart failure — obesity increases hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and left ventricular strain; cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in people with obesity.
Obstructive sleep apnoea — present in up to 40% of adults with obesity; causes daytime sleepiness, hypertension, and cardiovascular events; dramatically improves with weight loss.
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease progressing to steatohepatitis and cirrhosis — affects 30-40% of people with obesity and is now the leading indication for liver transplant in many countries.
Osteoarthritis — every 5 kg of weight loss reduces knee osteoarthritis symptoms by roughly equivalent to one NSAID; the mechanical load on weight-bearing joints is disproportionate to weight.

Certain cancers — obesity is a recognised risk factor for endometrial, postmenopausal breast, colorectal, oesophageal, kidney, and several other cancers, accounting for approximately 5% of cancers globally.

supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on causes, assessment, and treatment of obesity including referral to lifestyle services.

supportResources.name: CDC — Adult Obesity Facts
supportResources.description: U.S. statistics, health consequences, and policy context for adult obesity from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: WHO — Obesity and Overweight fact sheet
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, health consequences, and prevention strategies from the World Health Organization.

confusedWithConditions.name: Overweight
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Overweight (BMI 25-29.9 kg/m²) is distinguished from obesity by a lower BMI threshold; the clinical significance and comorbidity risk are substantially lower than in obesity, though risk increases progressively across the range.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of cardiometabolic abnormalities — central obesity plus at least two of raised blood pressure, dysglycaemia, high triglycerides, and low HDL — that frequently accompanies obesity but can occur at lower BMI and reflects visceral rather than total fat accumulation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Morbid (severe) obesity
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Morbid obesity (BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities) is a distinct severity class warranting consideration of bariatric surgery; it is not simply a quantitative extension of class I obesity but carries qualitatively higher comorbidity burden and treatment complexity.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Obesity is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes; weight loss of 5-10% reduces conversion from prediabetes by roughly 50%.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary artery disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Obesity drives hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and insulin resistance, which together accelerate atherosclerosis and coronary events.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Excess pharyngeal fat narrows the upper airway; OSA is present in up to 40% of adults with obesity and improves substantially with weight loss.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Mechanical loading from excess weight accelerates cartilage breakdown in weight-bearing joints; each kilogram of weight loss reduces knee joint load by roughly 4 kg.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Central obesity is the central diagnostic criterion of metabolic syndrome, linking excess abdominal fat to cardiometabolic risk clustering.

faqs.question: What BMI is considered obese?
faqs.answer: A body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or above is the standard definition of obesity in adults. Class I obesity spans 30-34.9, class II 35-39.9, and class III (severe) is 40 or above. BMI is calculated by dividing weight in kilograms by height in metres squared.

faqs.question: Is obesity a disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Major medical organisations including the American Medical Association, WHO, and NICE classify obesity as a chronic disease. This reflects that it results from complex biological, genetic, and environmental factors rather than being a simple lifestyle choice, and that it causes measurable organ dysfunction requiring medical management.

faqs.question: What causes obesity?
faqs.answer: Obesity results from sustained energy intake exceeding expenditure, driven by a combination of genetic predisposition, diet quality, physical inactivity, sleep deprivation, endocrine disorders, gut microbiome, stress, and weight-promoting medications. Ultra-processed food environments make it harder for most people to maintain energy balance, which is why obesity prevalence has risen in virtually every country.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for obesity?
faqs.answer: The most effective long-term treatment is bariatric surgery, achieving 25-35% weight loss with high rates of type 2 diabetes remission. Among non-surgical options, newer GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide (Wegovy) and tirzepatide achieve 15-22% weight loss in clinical trials. Lifestyle change alone typically produces 5-10% loss, which remains clinically meaningful but requires ongoing support to maintain.

faqs.question: How much weight do I need to lose to improve my health?
faqs.answer: Even a 5% reduction in body weight produces measurable benefits: blood pressure falls by 3-5 mmHg, risk of type 2 diabetes falls by roughly 50%, sleep apnoea severity reduces, and joint pain improves. A 10% loss amplifies these benefits further. The target is not a specific BMI but the smallest amount of weight loss that produces health improvement in the individual.

faqs.question: Are GLP-1 injections like Ozempic safe for weight loss?
faqs.answer: GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide (sold as Ozempic for diabetes, Wegovy for obesity) and tirzepatide are approved and effective for weight management in people with obesity. Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, and constipation, which are usually dose-dependent and temporary. They are not recommended in pregnancy or with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma.

faqs.question: Can obesity be cured?
faqs.answer: Obesity is a chronic relapsing disease. Bariatric surgery produces the most durable weight loss and can achieve long-term remission of type 2 diabetes and other comorbidities in many patients. Pharmacotherapy and lifestyle interventions require ongoing adherence; weight typically returns if treatment is stopped. The goal is long-term management rather than a one-time cure.

faqs.question: Does obesity always cause diabetes?
faqs.answer: No. Not all people with obesity develop type 2 diabetes, though the risk is approximately three times higher than in people of healthy weight. Roughly 30-40% of adults with class II obesity have diabetes. Risk depends on fat distribution, genetic susceptibility, and metabolic health. Early intervention with weight loss can prevent or delay diabetes indefinitely.

faqs.question: What are the health risks of obesity?
faqs.answer: Obesity is associated with type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, heart failure, obstructive sleep apnoea, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease progressing to cirrhosis, osteoarthritis, stroke, several cancers (endometrial, bowel, breast), mental health conditions, and reduced life expectancy. Risks increase with obesity class and with the duration of obesity.

faqs.question: How does bariatric surgery help with weight loss?
faqs.answer: Bariatric surgery changes the anatomy of the stomach and sometimes the intestine to reduce food intake and alter satiety hormones. Sleeve gastrectomy removes most of the stomach, reducing capacity and ghrelin levels. Gastric bypass creates a small stomach pouch and reroutes the intestine, altering both capacity and hormonal signalling. Both achieve 25-35% weight loss and high remission rates for type 2 diabetes.

faqs.question: Why is it so hard to keep weight off?
faqs.answer: When body weight falls, the body responds by increasing hunger hormones (ghrelin), decreasing satiety hormones (leptin, PYY), and lowering resting metabolic rate. These biological adaptations persist for years and strongly defend the prior higher weight. This is why long-term weight maintenance requires ongoing behavioural effort or sustained pharmacotherapy — not a lack of willpower.

faqs.question: Can exercise alone treat obesity?
faqs.answer: Exercise is essential for cardiovascular health, muscle preservation, and metabolic improvement, but it produces modest weight loss on its own — typically 2-3 kg over several months — because appetite often increases proportionally. Exercise combined with dietary change is far more effective than either alone, and exercise is the single strongest predictor of long-term weight maintenance.

faqs.question: Is obesity hereditary?
faqs.answer: Genetics account for an estimated 40-70% of BMI variation, based on twin studies. Hundreds of common genetic variants affecting appetite and energy regulation have been identified. Rare single-gene mutations cause severe early-onset obesity in a small minority. Genetics load the gun but environment pulls the trigger — people with genetic susceptibility benefit most from supportive environments.

faqs.question: What is the difference between obesity and being overweight?
faqs.answer: Overweight means a BMI of 25-29.9 kg/m² — above healthy weight but below the threshold for obesity. Obesity begins at BMI 30 and is subdivided into three classes by severity. Both carry increased health risks compared to a healthy BMI, but obesity, particularly class II and III, carries substantially higher comorbidity risk and typically warrants more intensive medical management.

faqs.question: Does obesity affect mental health?
faqs.answer: Obesity and mental health are bidirectionally linked. Depression and anxiety are 20-40% more common in people with obesity. The relationship runs both ways: depression reduces activity and increases emotional eating, while obesity-related stigma, pain, and reduced quality of life worsen mood. Treating mental health conditions is an important component of obesity management.

faqs.question: What foods cause obesity?
faqs.answer: No single food causes obesity, but diets dominated by ultra-processed foods — high in added sugar, refined starches, saturated fat, and engineered to override satiety — consistently drive excess calorie intake in population studies. Sugar-sweetened beverages are particularly associated with weight gain because liquid calories do not suppress hunger as effectively as solid food.

faqs.question: Can children have obesity?
faqs.answer: Yes. Childhood obesity is defined using age- and sex-specific BMI percentile charts rather than fixed adult cut-offs. Global childhood obesity rates have risen sharply since the 1980s. Children with obesity are at higher risk of adult obesity, early type 2 diabetes, and psychological difficulties. Treatment focuses on family-based lifestyle intervention rather than pharmacotherapy for most children.

faqs.question: How is obesity measured?
faqs.answer: The standard clinical measure is BMI — weight in kg divided by height in metres squared — with obesity defined as 30 or above. Waist circumference adds important information about central fat, which carries higher cardiometabolic risk. More precise measures of body fat percentage (DEXA scan, bioelectrical impedance) are used in research but not routinely in clinical practice.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about my weight?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if your BMI is 30 or above, if you have unexplained weight gain, or if you have weight-related symptoms such as breathlessness, joint pain, or snoring. Even at BMI 27-29 with diabetes, hypertension, or a family history of heart disease, a medical review is worthwhile. Early intervention is more effective than waiting until obesity is severe.

faqs.question: What is metabolic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of conditions — abdominal obesity, raised blood pressure, high blood sugar, high triglycerides, and low HDL cholesterol — that occur together and greatly increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes. At least three of the five criteria are required for diagnosis. Central obesity is its core driver, and weight loss addresses all five components.

faqs.question: Does sleep affect obesity?
faqs.answer: Yes. Short sleep duration, under seven hours, is independently associated with roughly 30% higher obesity risk. Sleep deprivation elevates ghrelin, suppresses leptin, increases appetite for calorie-dense foods, and reduces motivation to exercise. Treating obstructive sleep apnoea and improving sleep hygiene are therefore important components of obesity management.

metaDescription: Obesity affects 13% of adults worldwide and drives diabetes, heart disease and sleep apnoea. Learn BMI thresholds, causes, and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
